questions.question: What type of categorical proposition is this? "All birds are animals."
What type of categorical proposition is this? "No fish are mammals."
What type of categorical proposition is this? "Some students are not lazy."
What type of categorical proposition is this? "Some trees are tall."
Which of the following represents a universal affirmative proposition?
Which of the following represents a universal negative proposition?
Which of the following represents a particular affirmative proposition?
Convert the proposition "All squares are rectangles."
Convert the proposition "No mammals are cold-blooded."
Convert the proposition "Some birds are not carnivorous."
Obvert the proposition "All reptiles are vertebrates."
Obvert the proposition "Some trees are not deciduous."
What is the contradictory of "All dogs are friendly."?
What is the contrary of "No fruits are sour."?
If the universal affirmative proposition "All whales are marine animals." is false, what can we conclude?
options: Some whales are not marine animals.
All whales are not marine animals.
questions.answer_text: Some whales are not marine animals.

questions.question: If "No mammals lay eggs." is true, what is its subcontrary?
options: Some mammals do not lay eggs.
questions.answer_text: Some mammals do not lay eggs.

questions.question: Convert the proposition "Some birds are migratory."
options: Some migratory creatures are not birds.

questions.question: Convert the proposition "No snakes have legs."
options: Some creatures with legs are snakes.

questions.question: If "All humans are rational" is true, what can we infer about "Some humans are rational"?
What is the correct obversion of "Some cars are sports cars."?
options: Some cars are not sports cars.

questions.question: Convert the proposition "Some cats are not wild animals."
options: Some wild animals are not cats.

questions.question: If "All birds have feathers" is false, what can be inferred?
options: Some birds do not have feathers.
questions.answer_text: Some birds do not have feathers.

questions.question: Convert the proposition "No trees are animals."
If "Some planets are habitable" is false, what does it imply?
If "Some mammals are not carnivorous" is false, what can we conclude?
What type of categorical proposition is this? "Some books are fiction."
What type of categorical proposition is this? "No reptiles have fur."
What type of categorical proposition is this? "All students are not lazy."
Which of the following is an example of a particular negative proposition?
Convert the proposition "All triangles have three sides."
options: Some triangles do not have three sides.

questions.question: Convert the proposition "No cats are reptiles."
Convert the proposition "Some birds are not flightless."
Obvert the proposition "All snakes are reptiles."
Obvert the proposition "Some musicians are not classical artists."
What is the contradictory of "Some animals are not mammals."?
What is the contrary of "No humans are perfect."?
If the universal affirmative proposition "All computers have processors." is false, what can we conclude?
options: Some computers do not have processors.
questions.answer_text: Some computers do not have processors.

questions.question: If "No fish have lungs." is true, what is its subcontrary?
options: Some fish do not have lungs.
questions.answer_text: Some fish do not have lungs.

questions.question: Convert the proposition "Some scientists are Nobel laureates."
options: Some scientists are not Nobel laureates.

questions.question: Convert the proposition "No birds are insects."
Convert the proposition "Some flowers are not roses."
If "All astronauts are scientists" is true, what can we infer about "Some astronauts are scientists"?
What is the correct obversion of "Some musicians are composers."?
Convert the proposition "Some mammals are not marsupials."
If "All snakes are reptiles" is false, what does it imply?
Convert the proposition "No dolphins are fish."
If "Some planets have moons" is false, what does it imply?
options: Some planets do not have moons.

questions.question: If "Some birds are not carnivorous" is false, what can we conclude?
If the proposition "No mathematicians are poets" is true, what can be inferred about "Some mathematicians are poets"?
Which of the following best represents the obversion of "Some animals are reptiles"?
What is the subcontrary of "Some mammals are not carnivores"?
If the statement "All teachers are literate" is false, what must be true?
Convert the proposition "All whales are mammals" correctly.
If "Some birds can fly" is false, what must be true?
If "No lawyers are criminals" is true, which of the following is its correct conversion?
If "Some insects are not dangerous" is false, what must be true?
What is the contrapositive of "If it rains, the ground gets wet"?
options: If the ground is wet, then it has rained.
If the ground is not wet, then it has not rained.
If it does not rain, then the ground is not wet.
If the ground is wet, then it will rain.
questions.answer_text: If the ground is not wet, then it has not rained.

questions.question: Which of the following propositions contradicts "All dogs are friendly"?
Which of the following is an example of a particular affirmative proposition?
If "All engineers are problem-solvers" is true, which of the following is its correct conversion?
If "Some animals are not mammals" is false, what can we conclude?
Convert the proposition "No doctors are illiterate" correctly.
If "All metals conduct electricity" is true, what must be true?
options: Some metals do not conduct electricity.

questions.question: What is the obversion of "No cats are reptiles"?
What is the correct contrapositive of "If someone is a thief, then they are dishonest"?
options: If someone is not dishonest, then they are not a thief.
If someone is dishonest, then they are not a thief.
If someone is a thief, then they are honest.
If someone is not a thief, then they are dishonest.
questions.answer_text: If someone is not dishonest, then they are not a thief.

questions.question: The three laws of thought include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: The Law of the Excluded Middle

questions.question: Which of the following best defines a valid argument?
options: An argument where the premises and conclusion are all true
An argument where the premises logically lead to the conclusion
An argument that uses persuasive language
An argument that is widely accepted
questions.answer_text: An argument where the premises logically lead to the conclusion

questions.question: Which type of logic focuses on the structure of reasoning rather than the truth of the premises?
What is the key feature of a deductive argument?
options: It moves from specific cases to general principles
Its conclusion is only probable, not certain
If the premises are true, the conclusion must be true
It relies on examples and observation
questions.answer_text: If the premises are true, the conclusion must be true

questions.question: What distinguishes material logic from formal logic?
options: Material logic focuses on the truth of premises, while formal logic focuses on structure
Formal logic is concerned with argument content, while material logic checks validity
Material logic uses mathematical formulas, while formal logic does not
There is no difference between the two
questions.answer_text: Material logic focuses on the truth of premises, while formal logic focuses on structure

questions.question: If an argument is valid but has false premises, what can be said about it?
options: It is neither valid nor invalid
The conclusion must also be false

questions.question: The statement "If it is raining, then the ground is wet" is an example of:
What makes an argument sound?
options: The premises and conclusion are both true, regardless of validity
The argument is valid, and all premises are true
questions.answer_text: The argument is valid, and all premises are true

questions.question: What is the purpose of informal logic?
options: To analyze the structure of arguments without considering their truth
To study reasoning in everyday language and practical situations
To ensure that all arguments follow strict rules of inference
To examine only mathematical and symbolic reasoning
questions.answer_text: To study reasoning in everyday language and practical situations

questions.question: The Law of the Excluded Middle states that:
options: Every statement must be either true or false, with no middle ground
Two contradictory statements can both be true under certain conditions
The structure of an argument is more important than its content
A statement can be both true and false at the same time
questions.answer_text: Every statement must be either true or false, with no middle ground

questions.question: "All mammals are warm-blooded. Whales are mammals. Therefore, whales are warm-blooded." Which option correctly identifies the conclusion?
"In order to pass the test, you must study hard. John did not study hard. Therefore, John will not pass the test." What is the conclusion?
options: You must study hard to pass the test.
John will not pass the test.
Both A and B support the conclusion.
questions.answer_text: John will not pass the test.

questions.question: "All fruits have seeds. An apple is a fruit. Therefore, an apple has seeds." Identify the premises and the conclusion.
options: Premises: All fruits have seeds; Apple is a fruit. Conclusion: Apple has seeds.
Premise: Apple has seeds; Conclusion: All fruits have seeds.
Premise: Apple has seeds; Conclusion: Apple is a fruit.
Premises: All fruits have seeds; Apple has seeds. Conclusion: Apple is a fruit.
questions.answer_text: Premises: All fruits have seeds; Apple is a fruit. Conclusion: Apple has seeds.

questions.question: "Every student in the class passed the exam. Mary is a student in the class. Therefore, Mary passed the exam." Which part is the conclusion?
options: Every student in the class passed the exam.
Mary is a student in the class.

questions.question: "To win the game, you must practice regularly. Tom practices regularly. Therefore, Tom will win the game." What are the premises and what is the conclusion?
options: Premises: You must practice regularly; Tom practices regularly. Conclusion: Tom will win the game.
Premise: Tom will win the game. Conclusion: Tom practices regularly.
Premises: Tom practices regularly; Tom will win the game. Conclusion: You must practice regularly.
Premise: To win the game, you must practice regularly. Conclusion: Tom practices regularly.
questions.answer_text: Premises: You must practice regularly; Tom practices regularly. Conclusion: Tom will win the game.

questions.question: "If it is raining, then the picnic will be canceled. It is raining. Therefore, the picnic will be canceled." Identify the conclusion.
options: If it is raining, then the picnic will be canceled.

questions.question: "All dogs bark. Rex is a dog. Therefore, Rex barks." Which option shows the premises and the conclusion?
options: Premise: All dogs bark. Conclusion: Rex is a dog.
Premises: All dogs bark; Rex is a dog. Conclusion: Rex barks.
Premise: Rex barks. Conclusion: All dogs bark and Rex is a dog.
Premise: Rex is a dog. Conclusion: All dogs bark.
questions.answer_text: Premises: All dogs bark; Rex is a dog. Conclusion: Rex barks.

questions.question: "Studying daily improves your grades. Lisa studies daily. Therefore, Lisa's grades improve." What is the conclusion?
"If you water plants, they grow. The garden was watered. Therefore, the plants grew." Select the correct identification:
options: Premise: If you water plants, they grow; Garden was watered. Conclusion: Plants grew.
Premise: Plants grew; Conclusion: Garden was watered.
Premise: Garden was watered; Conclusion: If you water plants, they grow.
Premise: If you water plants, they grow; Conclusion: Garden was watered.
questions.answer_text: Premise: If you water plants, they grow; Garden was watered. Conclusion: Plants grew.

questions.question: "Everyone who studies passes the exam. Alex did not study. Therefore, Alex will not pass the exam." Identify the conclusion.
options: Everyone who studies passes the exam.
Alex will not pass the exam.
The argument has no clear conclusion.
questions.answer_text: Alex will not pass the exam.

questions.question: "All birds can fly. A penguin is a bird. Therefore, a penguin can fly." Is this argument:
questions.answer_text: Valid but unsound (The structure is valid, but the first premise is false.)

questions.question: "All mammals have hearts. A whale is a mammal. Therefore, a whale has a heart." This argument is:
questions.answer_text: Sound (Both premises and the conclusion are true, and the structure is valid.)

questions.question: "If it is snowing, then it is cold outside. It is snowing. Therefore, it is cold outside." This argument is:
questions.answer_text: Sound (The argument is valid and, assuming the premises are true, it is sound.)

questions.question: "If you study hard, you will get good grades. You did not study hard. Therefore, you will not get good grades." This argument is:
options: Sound if the conclusion is true
questions.answer_text: Invalid (This is an example of denying the antecedent.)

questions.question: "All cars have engines. My bicycle has an engine. Therefore, my bicycle is a car." This argument is:
questions.answer_text: Both unsound and invalid (The argument is invalid; the premises are also untrue regarding bicycles.)

questions.question: "Every student in the club is an honor student. Sarah is a student in the club. Therefore, Sarah is an honor student." The argument is:
questions.answer_text: Valid and sound (Assuming the premises are true, the structure is valid.)

questions.question: "If a person is a doctor, then they have a medical degree. John has a medical degree. Therefore, John is a doctor." This argument is:
questions.answer_text: Invalid (This is an example of affirming the consequent.)

questions.question: "All computers run on electricity. My laptop is a computer. Therefore, my laptop runs on electricity." This argument is:
options: Sound only if my laptop is plugged in
questions.answer_text: Sound (The structure is valid and the premises are true.)

questions.question: "Only mammals have fur. A dolphin is a mammal. Therefore, a dolphin has fur." This argument is:
questions.answer_text: Invalid (The argument incorrectly assumes that being a mammal implies having fur.)

questions.question: "If you exercise regularly, you will be healthy. You exercise regularly. Therefore, you are healthy." This argument is:
options: Sound only if no other factors affect health
questions.answer_text: Valid but unsound (The premise is not necessarily true because other factors influence health.)

questions.question: "All fish can fly. Goldie is a fish. Therefore, Goldie can fly." Which premise is problematic?
questions.answer_text: "All fish can fly" (The premise about fish flying is untrue.)

questions.question: "Every bird lays eggs. Penguins are birds. Therefore, penguins lay eggs." Which premise might be challenged if we consider an exception?
questions.answer_text: "Every bird lays eggs" (this premise can be scrutinized for exceptional cases.)

questions.question: Premise 1: All planets are round. Premise 2: Earth is a planet. Which premise could be disputed in certain philosophical discussions?
questions.answer_text: Premise 1 (Discussions about planetary shapes like oblate spheroid might dispute 'round' as oversimplified.)

questions.question: "All vehicles have four wheels. My motorcycle is a vehicle. Therefore, my motorcycle has four wheels." Which premise is untrue?
questions.answer_text: "All vehicles have four wheels" (Motorcycles are vehicles but typically have two wheels.)

questions.question: "Every computer program is written in a high-level language." If some programs are in assembly language, which premise may be false?
options: "Every computer program is written in a high-level language"
"This software is written in a high-level language"
questions.answer_text: "Every computer program is written in a high-level language"

questions.question: "If a person is a millionaire, then they are happy. John is not happy. Therefore, John is not a millionaire." Which premise is likely questionable?
options: "If a person is a millionaire, then they are happy"
questions.answer_text: "If a person is a millionaire, then they are happy" (The idea that all millionaires are happy is a generalization.)

questions.question: "All fruits are sweet. Lemons are fruits. Therefore, lemons are sweet." Which premise is problematic?
questions.answer_text: "All fruits are sweet" (Not all fruits are sweet; lemons are sour.)

questions.question: "If a car is new, then it runs smoothly. This car is new. Therefore, it runs smoothly." If the car has a manufacturing defect, which element is misleading?
options: "If a car is new, then it runs smoothly"
questions.answer_text: "If a car is new, then it runs smoothly" (The conditional is misleading if not all new cars run smoothly.)

questions.question: "Every student who studies passes the exam. Emily is a student. Emily passed the exam." Which premise might be considered unverified?
options: "Every student who studies passes the exam"
questions.answer_text: "Every student who studies passes the exam" (This universal statement can be challenged in real-life situations.)

questions.question: "If it rains, the streets get wet. The streets are wet. Therefore, it rained." Which premise or assumption is problematic?
options: "If it rains, the streets get wet"
The argument wrongly assumes no other cause for wet streets.
questions.answer_text: The argument wrongly assumes no other cause for wet streets (ignores causes like street cleaning or a sprinkler.)

questions.question: Identify the inductive argument:
options: If it rains, the ground gets wet. It rained. Therefore, the ground is wet.
All squares have four sides. A rectangle is a square. Therefore, a rectangle has four sides.
Most dogs bark when they see strangers. My neighbor's dog is barking. Therefore, it probably sees a stranger.
If an animal is a bird, it must lay eggs. A parrot is a bird. Therefore, it lays eggs.
questions.answer_text: Most dogs bark... (The conclusion is based on probability rather than certainty.)

questions.question: Which argument is deductive?
options: The last five times I drank coffee, I felt more awake. Therefore, drinking coffee always makes people more awake.
Every prime number greater than 2 is odd. 13 is a prime number greater than 2. Therefore, 13 is odd.
All swans I've seen are white. Therefore, all swans must be white.
The majority of past presidents have been male. Therefore, the next president will likely be male.
questions.answer_text: Every prime number greater than 2 is odd... (It follows strict logical necessity.)

questions.question: Identify the inductive argument:
options: If it is raining, then the streets will be wet. It is raining. Therefore, the streets are wet.
Every action has an equal and opposite reaction. A rocket engine pushes down, so the rocket moves up.
All cars I have ever seen have four wheels. Therefore, all cars must have four wheels.
Every bachelor is an unmarried man. John is a bachelor. Therefore, John is unmarried.
questions.answer_text: All cars I have ever seen have four wheels... (Based on observations but not necessarily always true.)

questions.question: Which of the following is deductive?
options: Most cats are independent. My friend has a cat. Therefore, my friend's cat is probably independent.
Every person who studies hard passes the exam. Mary studied hard. Therefore, Mary passed the exam.
The past five winters have been colder than average. Therefore, next winter will likely be colder.
90% of the students in this school have smartphones. John is a student here, so he probably has a smartphone.
questions.answer_text: Every person who studies hard passes the exam... (It follows a strict logical rule.)

questions.question: Which argument is inductive?
options: No triangles have four sides. Shape X is a triangle. Therefore, shape X does not have four sides.
All people born in Canada are Canadian citizens. Sarah was born in Canada. Therefore, Sarah is a Canadian citizen.
Every swan I have seen is white. Therefore, the next swan I see will probably be white.
If an animal is a fish, then it breathes through gills. A shark is a fish. Therefore, it breathes through gills.
questions.answer_text: Every swan I have seen is white... (Based on observations and probability.)

questions.question: What makes an argument deductive?
options: It draws conclusions based on probability and past experience.
It guarantees the truth of the conclusion if the premises are true.
It makes a generalization based on specific observations.
It assumes that the past will always predict the future.
questions.answer_text: It guarantees the truth of the conclusion if the premises are true.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of inductive reasoning?
options: All birds have feathers. A robin is a bird. Therefore, a robin has feathers.
No human can survive without oxygen. John is a human. Therefore, John needs oxygen to live.
My grandfather smoked and lived to 95. Therefore, smoking does not always cause early death.
If today is Tuesday, then tomorrow is Wednesday. Today is Tuesday. Therefore, tomorrow is Wednesday.
questions.answer_text: My grandfather smoked and lived to 95... (Makes a probable generalization, not a strict logical necessity.)

questions.question: A deductive argument is one in which:
options: The conclusion follows with certainty if the premises are true.
The conclusion is always true, even if the premises are false.
The conclusion is only probable, not certain.
The argument is based entirely on past observations.
questions.answer_text: The conclusion follows with certainty if the premises are true.

questions.question: Which of the following best represents inductive reasoning?
options: All men are mortal. Socrates is a man. Therefore, Socrates is mortal.
The last five times I went jogging, I felt great afterward. Therefore, jogging always makes people feel great.
If a shape is a square, then it has four equal sides. Shape X is a square. Therefore, it has four equal sides.
If a student studies, they pass the test. Mike studied. Therefore, he passed the test.
questions.answer_text: The last five times I went jogging... (The conclusion is based on repeated observations, but not necessarily true for all cases.)

questions.question: Which branch of philosophy deals with the study of knowledge—its nature, sources, and validity?
Which statement best captures René Descartes's view of metaphysics?
options: It is the study of values and moral judgments.
It is primarily concerned with linguistic analysis and the structure of arguments.
It is considered the "first philosophy," dealing with the fundamental nature of reality and existence.
It is purely concerned with political structures and human relationships.
questions.answer_text: It is considered the "first philosophy," dealing with the fundamental nature of reality and existence.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the traditionally recognized major branches of philosophy?
In the context of epistemology, which source of knowledge involves divine undertones and communication from God?
According to the text, logic is primarily concerned with which of the following?
options: The study of moral values and judgments
The methods and principles used in distinguishing correct from incorrect reasoning
The study of the origin, nature, and development of the universe
The study of the aesthetic values in art and nature
questions.answer_text: The methods and principles used in distinguishing correct from incorrect reasoning

questions.question: Axiology, one of the major branches of philosophy, is subdivided mainly into:
Which philosopher warned that "If the world's ethical standards fail to rise to the advance of our technological revolution, we shall perish"?
What does the text describe as the "king" of all disciplines, which leads to the highest degree in many fields (Ph.D.)?
Which branch of philosophy studies "the nature of reality," dealing with questions such as the origin of the universe, the nature of existence, and the mind-body relationship?
According to the text, which of the following is NOT typically a direct source of knowledge mentioned?
Which branch of philosophy is primarily focused on the formal principles of reasoning, argument structure, and the evaluation of inferences?
Which sub-branch of axiology is concerned with determining the criteria for what is considered beautiful or artistically valuable?
Which philosophical method, famously associated with René Descartes, involves doubting all beliefs until reaching an undeniable foundation?
The statement "I think, therefore I am" is most closely associated with which philosopher?
Which of the following sources of knowledge is based on direct sensory experience?
In the context of the text, which branch of philosophy would include the study of moral dilemmas and questions about right and wrong?
Which of the following best describes the relationship between philosophy and other academic disciplines?
options: Philosophy is a specialized field with no connection to other disciplines.
Philosophy is the "mother of the sciences," forming the foundation for various academic fields and the Ph.D. degree.
Philosophy only concerns abstract ideas with no practical application.
Philosophy primarily deals with historical analysis rather than scientific inquiry.
questions.answer_text: Philosophy is the "mother of the sciences," forming the foundation for various academic fields and the Ph.D. degree.

questions.question: Which source of knowledge is associated with gaining insight without a deliberate, conscious process?
Which major branch of philosophy deals with questions about the nature of existence, reality, and the universe?
Which branch of philosophy is primarily concerned with studying the nature and existence of the universe (cosmology), and from which major branch is it deduced?
Which branch of philosophy deals with the systematic study of valid reasoning and argument structure, and what is its primary purpose?
options: Logic; to distinguish correct from incorrect reasoning
Epistemology; to analyze the sources of knowledge
Metaphysics; to explore the nature of being
questions.answer_text: Logic; to distinguish correct from incorrect reasoning

questions.question: The study of moral values, ethical dilemmas, and aesthetic judgments is grouped under which branch of philosophy?
options: Metaphysics; to understand the nature of existence
Epistemology; to question the basis of knowledge
Axiology; to assess what is valuable and good
questions.answer_text: Axiology; to assess what is valuable and good

questions.question: Which branch of philosophy is dedicated to understanding the nature, sources, and limits of human knowledge?
options: Epistemology; it investigates sense perception, reason, intuition, and revelation
Metaphysics; it investigates the origin of the universe
Logic; it investigates the structure of arguments
Axiology; it investigates moral and aesthetic values
questions.answer_text: Epistemology; it investigates sense perception, reason, intuition, and revelation

questions.question: Which branch of philosophy lays the groundwork for scientific inquiry and is sometimes referred to as the "mother of the sciences"?
options: Philosophy as a whole (with emphasis on Metaphysics)
questions.answer_text: Philosophy as a whole (with emphasis on Metaphysics)

questions.question: In the context of evaluating arguments, which branch of philosophy helps to identify fallacies and ensure logical consistency?
options: Epistemology; to test the reliability of sensory data
Logic; to distinguish between valid and invalid reasoning
Metaphysics; to explain the nature of reality
Axiology; to define what is ethically right
questions.answer_text: Logic; to distinguish between valid and invalid reasoning

questions.question: Which branch of philosophy is directly concerned with questions about existence and being, and how does it relate to the study of the cosmos?
options: Epistemology; it relates by explaining how we come to know cosmic phenomena
Axiology; it relates by judging the beauty of the cosmos
Metaphysics; it relates by addressing the fundamental principles that govern existence, including the cosmos
Logic; it relates by structuring the arguments about cosmic theories
questions.answer_text: Metaphysics; it relates by addressing the fundamental principles that govern existence, including the cosmos

questions.question: Who was the first philosopher in the cosmology group?
What did Thales of Miletus consider as the basic principle underlying reality?
Thales of Miletus is remembered for predicting what astronomical event?
In which century did Thales of Miletus live?
What date is generally agreed upon for the solar eclipse predicted by Thales?
Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a pre-Socratic philosopher?
What term is used to describe the philosophers of the pre-Socratic period?
What was the main interest of the pre-Socratic philosophers mentioned in the text?
options: Discovering the basic nature of reality
questions.answer_text: Discovering the basic nature of reality

questions.question: Who is considered the father of modern philosophy?
Which philosopher argued that man should focus on self-knowledge rather than searching for ultimate reality?
Which era came after the Classical Socratic or Platonic Era?
What period was known as the Golden Age of Greece?
Which philosopher is credited with the phrase "knowledge is power"?
What institution played a dominant role in Medieval Philosophy?
Which period of philosophy sought to reconcile faith and reason?
Which Arabic philosopher contributed to Medieval Philosophy?
What period saw the need to reconcile science with philosophy?
Who was called "the trumpeter of the new science"?

categories.name: Early Modern and Late Modern Philosophy

questions.question: Which philosopher is famous for the phrase "I think, therefore I am"?
Which two philosophical schools were prominent in the 16th and 17th centuries?
Which philosopher wrote "The Leviathan"?
Who is credited with reconciling rationalism and empiricism?
Who was a disciple of Hegel and applied dialectics to economics?
